behaviors.description: Out-of-stock products are excluded from recommendation results regardless of relevance score.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is entirely within the availability filter function — given a list of products with stock flags, it must exclude out-of-stock items. No module boundary is crossed. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess. MOCK-HIDE: An in-process list of products with stock status fully exercises the filter. LLM-DEP: No ML inference involved. REJECTION: Integration rejected — the failure mode is a single filter predicate, not a handoff between modules.
behaviors.test_description: Construct an in-memory product list where items A (in-stock, low score) and B (out-of-stock, high score) exist. Pass the list through the availability filter. Assert item B is absent from the output regardless of its relevance score, and item A is present.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives in the preference filter function — given blocked categories from user settings and a product list, it must exclude matching products. REAL-INFRA: User settings and catalog can both be in-process data structures. MOCK-HIDE: A real user settings object (or stub) with a known blocked list fully exercises the logic. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION: Integration rejected — there is no inter-module state handoff required to expose the failure; the filter logic is self-contained.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user settings object with blocked categories ['electronics', 'toys']. Build a product catalog containing items from 'electronics', 'toys', and 'clothing'. Run the preference filter. Assert no products from 'electronics' or 'toys' appear in the output, and 'clothing' products are retained.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products matching the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the scoring/ranking function's weighting logic — a product in a recently browsed category must score higher than one with no browsing overlap. This is a deterministic algorithmic property testable with controlled inputs. REAL-INFRA: No external I/O needed. LLM-DEP: The algorithm is described as rule-based weighting of signals, not LLM inference — the expected output is deterministic given known inputs. REJECTION: Agentic rejected — no non-deterministic model inference is required to test that browsing signal raises rank.
behaviors.test_description: Provide the ranking algorithm with a fixed user browsing history (category 'shoes', 3 views) and two candidate products: P1 in category 'shoes' and P2 in category 'garden'. Invoke the ranker and assert P1 receives a strictly higher rank position than P2.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if underlying data is unchanged.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the pagination function's slice logic and the sort stability of the ranking step. Both can be exercised with a fixed in-memory ranked list. REAL-INFRA: No filesystem, network, or subprocess. MOCK-HIDE: A deterministic in-memory list fully exposes any instability or off-by-one in page slicing. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION: Integration rejected — stable sort and correct page slicing are pure algorithmic properties; no cross-module state is required.
behaviors.test_description: Build a fixed ranked list of 25 products. Call the paginator with page_size=10, page=2 twice consecutively on the same input. Assert both calls return the same 10 product IDs in the same order, and that the IDs are exactly positions 11–20 of the original ranked list.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement).
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The serendipity logic is a property of the ranker or post-processing step — it must inject diversity by including at least one unexplored category. With controlled input (known browsed categories, catalog covering additional categories), this is fully testable. REAL-INFRA: No external I/O. LLM-DEP: The serendipity rule is a deterministic constraint, not an ML inference quality judgment. REJECTION: Agentic rejected — the test checks presence of a rule outcome, not quality of an ML ranking; the pass/fail criterion is binary and deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Set user browsing history to categories ['A', 'B', 'C']. Provide a catalog containing products in categories A, B, C, D, and E. Run the recommendation algorithm. Assert that the output list contains at least one product whose category is not in ['A', 'B', 'C'].

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives popularity-based recommendations rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The cold-start failure — returning an empty list instead of popularity fallback — only appears when the recommendation service is wired to the popularity data source. If the popularity store returns an empty stub, the bug (no fallback wiring) is hidden. The failure mode is at the boundary between the recommendation orchestrator and the popularity ranking module/store. REAL-INFRA: A real popularity data source is not required; a local fake DB or in-process stub popularity repository is sufficient. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION: Unit rejected — a unit test mocking the popularity source would pass even if the real wiring never calls the fallback path.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a local fake popularity store with product ranking data. Create a new user with empty purchase and browsing history. Call the recommendation service end-to-end. Assert the response is non-empty and that the returned products match the top-ranked items from the popularity store, not an empty list or error.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure could be that the click event handler writes incorrect field values or fails to persist to the tracking store. This only surfaces when the handler is wired to the persistence layer. A Unit test mocking the store write would not catch serialization bugs, field mapping errors, or incorrect timestamp capture. REAL-INFRA: A real external service is not needed; a local in-memory or embedded tracking store suffices. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION: Unit rejected — mocking the store always passes even when the actual data written is wrong.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the click tracking handler to a local in-memory tracking store. Simulate a click on product ID 'P42' at list position 3 at a known timestamp T. After the event, query the tracking store. Assert exactly one record exists with product_id='P42', position=3, and timestamp within 1 second of T.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Wall-clock latency at scale depends on real execution characteristics — DB query plans, network round trips, index behavior, and process overhead — that in-process fakes cannot replicate. An in-memory mock catalog of 100k items would give misleadingly fast results hiding real bottlenecks in serialization, DB scanning, or inter-service calls. MOCK-HIDE: Any mocked data layer will always satisfy 200ms trivially, hiding the real failure. REJECTION: Integration rejected — performance assertions at this scale require the real execution stack; no in-process wiring test can falsify the 200ms bound meaningfully.
behaviors.test_description: Deploy the recommendation service against a real (or realistic) catalog store pre-loaded with 100,000 products. Issue a recommendation request for a user with typical browsing history. Measure wall-clock end-to-end response time using a real HTTP client. Assert the response is received within 200ms. Run at least 10 trials and assert the p95 latency is under 200ms.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure — purchased product still ranking highly — only appears when the recommendation orchestrator reads the purchase history store and the ranker applies the deprioritization rule across separate calls. A Unit test of just the ranker with injected purchase history would not catch a bug where the purchase event is never persisted or never read back. REAL-INFRA: A local fake purchase history store suffices; no real external service required. LLM-DEP: The deprioritization rule is deterministic. REJECTION: Unit rejected — the cross-call state dependency (write purchase, then read in next recommendation request) requires module wiring, not just isolated function logic.
behaviors.test_description: Using a local fake purchase store and recommendation service wired together: first request recommendations for user U and record product P's initial rank. Then record a purchase event for product P by user U into the fake store. Request recommendations again. Assert product P's rank is strictly lower than its initial rank, or that it is absent from the top-N results.

behaviors.description: Recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Determinism across two calls requires that the state-reading layer (purchase history, browsing log, catalog) is consistently queried. If any module introduces randomness or non-deterministic ordering, it only surfaces when two real calls read from the same underlying state. A Unit test of the ranker alone with the same injected input trivially returns the same output. REAL-INFRA: No external process needed; an in-process fake store is sufficient. LLM-DEP: The algorithm is described as deterministic weighting, not LLM inference; if it were LLM-based, the level would be Agentic. REJECTION: Agentic rejected — no non-deterministic model call is described; the determinism requirement applies to the pipeline's data access and ranking logic.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a local fake store with a fixed user state (known browsing history, purchase history, catalog). Call the recommendation service twice with the same user ID and no writes between calls. Assert the two response lists are identical in product IDs and in rank order.
